flexbox_basics.title: Flexbox Basics
flexbox_basics.subtitle: The layout system behind every React Native UI

body.heading1: How Flexbox works in React Native
body.text1: React Native uses Flexbox as its default layout engine. Unlike the web, the default `flexDirection` is `'column'`, so children stack vertically. Every `View` is already a flex container — no need to set `display: 'flex'`.
body.text2: `justifyContent` aligns children along the main axis and `alignItems` aligns them along the cross axis. With the default `'column'` direction, `justifyContent: 'center'` centers vertically and `alignItems: 'center'` centers horizontally. Switch to `flexDirection: 'row'` and the axes flip.
body.text3: Set `flex: 1` on a child to make it fill all available space on the main axis. When multiple children carry `flex` values, the space is divided proportionally. A child without `flex` keeps its natural size.

positioning.title: Absolute Positioning
positioning.subtitle: Overlapping elements with position and zIndex

body.heading1: Taking an element out of the flow
body.text1: Setting `position: 'absolute'` removes an element from the normal layout flow. It is then placed relative to its nearest ancestor with a defined size. Use `top`, `bottom`, `left`, and `right` to offset the element from each edge.
body.text2: When elements overlap, `zIndex` controls the stacking order — a higher value renders on top. In React Native, `zIndex` only works reliably between siblings that share the same parent.
